turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some really bad stomach issues - crampy lower abdominal pain mostly on the left side, and my bowel movements have gotten way worse. I'm talking 8 to 10 times a day, and it's just loose watery stuff that's really urgent.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It's not just the frequency or the looseness of my stool - it's the pain that comes with it. I feel like I'm always on edge, waiting for my next bowel movement because it feels like it's going to be a disaster, and then when it happens, I'm in agony with these cramps.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yeah, definitely. My heart starts racing, my palms get sweaty, and I feel really exhausted afterwards. It's like I've been running a marathon, but I haven't done anything physical all day. The fatigue is what's really getting to me - it's affecting my work performance at the startup where I work as a software engineer. I'm usually someone who can handle stress well, but this is just wearing me down.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: Yeah, like I said, my palms get sweaty. It's definitely noticeable during those episodes, and it lingers for a bit after they're over too.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: Only on my palms, yeah. My whole body doesn't break out in sweat or anything like that, just my palms get really clammy.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain is mostly localized to the left lower abdomen, but sometimes it radiates to the middle and a bit to the right side, making it hard for me to pinpoint exactly where it's centered. It feels like it's moving under my skin, but the cramping is usually most intense on the left side.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: The pain and discomfort don't really radiate to any other areas like my arms, neck, or jaw. It mostly stays confined to my lower abdomen, with some occasional movement to nearby areas, but nothing that would be considered a referred pain in those regions.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, I don't feel any tingling or numbness in my arms during these episodes. To be honest, my main concern is the impact this is having on my work and overall well-being - I'm starting to feel like I'm losing control of my life because of this constant discomfort and fatigue.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, I don't feel any tingling or numbness in my arms during these episodes. My main concern is the impact this is having on my work and overall well-being - I'm starting to feel like I'm losing control of my life because of this constant discomfort and fatigue.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: Actually, it's been a bit concerning me lately that my knees and ankles have been feeling achy, but there's no swelling or redness. I've just been attributing it to overworking and stress, but maybe it's related to what's going on with my stomach? It feels like everything is connected somehow.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, my shoes don't feel any tighter than usual, and I haven't noticed any puffiness in my legs. To be honest, it feels like every part of my body is connected to this stomach issue somehow – I've been trying to brush off the knee and ankle pain as just stress or overwork, but now that you mention it, maybe there's something more going on.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Yeah, the nausea is a big part of it. I get this awful feeling like I'm going to throw up right before I have a bowel movement, and sometimes I even do feel queasy after it's over. It's not just the frequency or pain that's getting to me, but also this constant feeling of being on edge, waiting for the next episode to happen.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, I get this awful feeling like I'm going to throw up right before I have a bowel movement. It's really intense and it lingers for a bit after it's over too. The nausea is a big part of what's making me feel so anxious and on edge - I just don't know when the next episode will hit or how bad it'll be.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: I've been running a low-grade fever, nothing too high, but it's definitely been there - 37.8 to 38 degrees Celsius, just a few times a week. It's not every day, but it's enough that I can feel it, you know? And to be honest, the night sweats have been pretty bad lately, waking me up from sleep 2-3 times per night because of diarrhea.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've actually lost a bit of weight over the past few weeks. I'd say it's around 4 kilograms, which is noticeable even to myself. I'm not really eating much because the thought of food makes me anxious and worried about when my next episode will hit. It's like my body is always in this state of high alert, waiting for the next bowel movement, so appetite has been a real challenge lately.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: My diet and exercise habits have actually changed significantly over the past few months due to my work schedule. As a software engineer at a startup, I've been working long hours, often 60+ hour weeks. Because of that, I've been relying heavily on takeout and fast food for meals, which isn't really ideal. And when I do have time to cook, it's usually something quick and easy, not necessarily balanced or nutritious. Exercise-wise, I try to get in some form of physical activity during my lunch break or after work, but even that has been inconsistent lately due to the fatigue and discomfort from my symptoms.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: Honestly, I try to get in some form of physical activity during my lunch break or after work, but even that has been inconsistent lately due to the fatigue and discomfort from my symptoms. When I'm feeling up to it, I'll go for a walk around the block or do some light stretching at home, but anything more strenuous than that is just too much to handle right now.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, my diet has been pretty irregular due to work hours. I usually grab something quick and easy in the morning before heading out, whether it's takeout or fast food on the go. Lunch is often at my desk while I'm working, sometimes a sandwich from the deli downstairs or leftovers from last night's dinner. Dinners are typically takeout or more takeout because by the time I get home, I'm exhausted and don't feel like cooking. Sometimes I'll stop by a convenience store on my way home for a snack if I need something to hold me over until bedtime. I try to have some fruit or nuts around the apartment as a healthier option, but it's not always easy with my schedule.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's about all I know about our family's history of GI issues - it hasn't really affected anyone else to my knowledge.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. That's a pretty significant condition that comes to mind when I think about potential family history of GI issues.
